summary: As governor of Alabama, I have seen firsthand the impact our administration has had on the state's industrial growth, education, and public health. Since 1970, we've expanded educational opportunities with junior colleges and medical schools, graduating more doctors annually. Alabama has maintained low property taxes, making it affordable for residents. Our people are proud, overcoming historical economic hardships and stereotypes to build a better future.

The University of Alabama has been central to our efforts to improve education and healthcare in the state. We established a medical school there and family physician programs at its branches, aiming to graduate more doctors to serve Alabama. This expansion reflects our commitment to accessible higher education and public health improvements during my governorship starting in 1970.

I have observed that many people felt the Democratic Party in 1972 was aloof and disconnected from the concerns of the middle class and broader public. The party's platform then did not resonate with many voters, contributing to a sense of government being out of touch. Despite this, I have worked within the party framework to represent the views of my constituents in Alabama and beyond.

Gallup polls have shown that a significant majority of people, both black and white, opposed busing policies in schools during the early 1970s. This data reflected the public's frustration with government mandates that seemed disconnected from their wishes. Such polling helped illustrate the broader sentiment of many Alabamians and Americans who felt government was imposing unpopular decisions.

President Roosevelt once described Alabama in 1932 as the number one economic problem of the nation, which was an accurate reflection of the hardships we faced. His era highlighted the struggles of the South recovering from the Civil War and economic restrictions like freight rate inequities. Despite these challenges, the people of Alabama showed great pride and resilience in rebuilding their communities.

I have often contrasted Alabama with places like Michigan to emphasize that people in all regions want prosperity for each other. While Michigan and Alabama differ economically and culturally, there is mutual respect and a shared hope for progress. This comparison helps illustrate the broader national context in which Alabama's development occurs.

The Pittsburgh Plus freight rate system was one of the economic restrictions that held back Alabama and the South after the Civil War. It artificially increased shipping costs, making it difficult for our agricultural economy to compete and contributing to outmigration in the 1920s and 1930s. Overcoming such barriers has been part of Alabama's long struggle for economic progress.

Unlike Europe, the South, including Alabama, did not receive Marshall Plan aid after World War II to rebuild its destroyed infrastructure. Instead, we faced occupation and economic hardships without such assistance. Despite this, the people of Alabama managed a remarkable comeback, rebuilding their communities and economy under very difficult circumstances.

During and after World War II, the South did not benefit from Lend-Lease aid like other regions, which hampered our recovery. Our railroads, schools, and livestock were devastated, and people struggled to survive day to day. The resilience of Alabama's people in overcoming these challenges is a testament to their strength and pride.

Senator Robert F. Kennedy was among the national figures who visited Alabama and engaged with its people during my time as governor. His presence, along with other leaders, helped bring attention to our state and its progress. I have always seen myself as a representative of the people, and visits from leaders like Senator Kennedy reinforced that connection.

Hubert Humphrey was another prominent national figure who came to Alabama during my tenure as governor. His visits signified recognition of our state's importance and the changes underway here. I often referenced leaders like Humphrey to show how Alabama was part of the broader national political conversation.

I was invited to speak at events far from Alabama, including at places like Madison Square Garden, reflecting the reach of my political influence beyond the South. Although my physical condition sometimes prevented me from attending, such invitations showed that the views I represented had national significance. It was a sign of how Alabama's voice was being heard across the country.

During the 1968 presidential election, Alabama and the South were often portrayed as backward, but I knew the reality was much more complex. Our region had overcome tremendous economic and social challenges, and the election highlighted the evolving political landscape. I used this period to travel and communicate the pride and progress of Alabama to the rest of the country.

In the 1972 presidential election, I was actively involved and even invited to campaign in states far from Alabama. That year, many felt the Democratic Party was out of touch, which influenced voter sentiment. My role as governor gave me a platform to represent the concerns of my constituents during this pivotal election cycle.
